title: NOT HERE (subtitle: "Last Stop on the Owl Line")
logline: You step off the 11:47 night train into Meridian — a town the maps forgot, where fog unmakes a street every night and everyone has been waiting for you, though no one can say your name. A branching elegy about what you carry when you can't keep everything.
setting: Meridian, a former railway junction town, population sign reading "pop. —" (the number faded). Bypassed by the interstate and struck from state maps in June 1974, it now exists only between: the 11:47 train still stops there nightly, though it appears on no schedule, and radio station KNWH ("K-Nowhere") broadcasts to a dial position that doesn't exist. Every calendar in town reads June 1974. Every night, the fog takes another street — buildings go "thin," words for things stop working, and the residents have normalized it completely ("Third Street's gone quiet again; take Cooper instead, love"). The atmosphere is Kentucky Route Zero's magical-realist melancholy crossed with the domestic warmth of a diner at 2am: sodium lamps, moth-colored fog, one lit window at the Anchor & Lantern inn, a piano being played badly on purpose. WHY IT FITS BROWSER + TERMINAL: the entire game is diegetically framed as Meridian's night dispatch — the browser build renders the town's radio/teletype log with warm phosphor glow and full sampled score; the terminal build IS the transmitter, the same text arriving as teletype with the score's chip-synth "AM broadcast version." The fog mechanic is native to text: fading streets literally render fainter, and corrupted memory renders as corrupted glyphs. A text game about a place being erased is a game where erasing text is the horror.
protagonist: You arrive on the 11:47 with a ticket you don't remember buying, a suitcase that isn't yours, and no name — the save name you typed at the start is never spoken by anyone; residents call you "the guest," "kiddo," "love." Voice: second-person present tense, spare and close ("You knock. The dog that is sometimes called Biscuit does not bark. Nothing here barks."). You believe you are a stranger, possibly the "surveyor" the town has been waiting decades for. You are wrong. The suitcase is yours — packed the morning of the day you left Meridian at age nine, in June 1974, and never came back. The protagonist is a rememberer at the end of their life, though the game never crassly cuts to a hospital bed; it stays inside the fog's logic. Character creation happens diegetically as the Ticket Interview: the Conductor punches your ticket while asking five strange questions ("What did mornings smell like, where you're from?"), and your answers become your stats and three "memory tokens" — nouns that will be waiting for you, verbatim, inside the ninth room in Act III.
statsDesign: Four stats plus one dynamic meter, all qualities of a REMEMBERER, not an adventurer. (1) CLARITY — how sharply you remember. Gates noticing clues, reading faded text (low Clarity literally renders some passages illegible), recalling earlier scenes verbatim in dialogue, and hearing buried instrument layers in the score. (2) WARMTH — how you were loved and how you love. Gates confidences: Mercy finishing her sentences, Elias handing you the map, the dog following you. The "kind" options aren't free — they require Warmth earned by doing small unglamorous things (washing dishes, walking the border, listening to the whole song). (3) WEIGHT — the grief and guilt you carry. Not a dump stat and not purely bad: some thin places only admit those who carry Weight ("the fog respects a full suitcase"), but high Weight slows you — you get fewer actions per night. (4) NERVE — willingness to look directly at hard things. Gates the Dora Vex truth-path (learning the twist mid-game), opening the pencil door, and staying in a scene when the game itself suggests you leave. (5) STATIC (meter, 0–100) — the fog's claim on you. Rises when you lie, force, or look away; falls when you name things honestly. High Static detunes and bitcrushes the score in real time, corrupts your own dialogue options into glyph-rot, and gates you OUT of the best endings. Replay pull: builds are real — a high-Nerve/low-Warmth run learns the truth in Act II and plays a completely different back half than a high-Warmth run that stays innocent until the ninth room.

cast.name: Mercy Halloran
cast.role: Innkeeper of the Anchor & Lantern, the last lit building in town; feeds everyone, keeps the lamp burning 'until the surveyor comes.' Functionally your home base and the game's warmth.
cast.want: To keep the lamp lit and the guest fed — to hold the center of town one more night, every night.
cast.secret: She is your mother as you remembered her at 34 — and she has begun to suspect what she is, ever since she found the inn's ninth room and saw that its door is only drawn in pencil. She knows who you are from the first night and hides it to spare you the weeks you have left.
cast.reactsToMemory: If you lie to her even once, she stops finishing her sentences around you for the rest of the game — trailing off, 'well. You know.' — because a memory can't hold words its rememberer poisoned. If instead you helped wash dishes on the first night (a nothing choice, easily skipped), she gives you the tin whistle's missing note in Act III, without which the 'Kept' ending's final chord cannot resolve.

cast.name: Mr. Sett, the Conductor
cast.role: Runs the 11:47. Courteous, exactly punctual, never removes his gloves. The only door out of Meridian, and the game's quiet antagonist-who-isn't.
cast.want: To keep the schedule: deliver you on time. He has bent the rules to give you this stopover at all, and it is costing him something he won't name.
cast.secret: He is not a memory. He is the only other real presence in Meridian — a psychopomp who has done this ten thousand times and, for reasons in his brass ledger, chose to give you nine nights when the schedule allowed none.
cast.reactsToMemory: He records every kindness you do in a brass ledger, silently, including ones no other character witnessed. At the final platform scene he reads the ledger's count aloud — and the number of entries determines whether he offers you his gloved hand or, for the first time in ten thousand runs, removes the glove.

cast.name: Wren Adelaide
cast.role: Fifteen-year-old overnight operator of KNWH, broadcasting to nobody. Sardonic, fearless, never sleeps. The game's musical heart — dedications, requests, the songs that cut off mid-bridge.
cast.want: One signal back. A single caller, a single letter, proof that someone outside the fog can hear her.
cast.secret: She is your older sister, who died the spring before your family left Meridian — the reason you left. She is frozen at fifteen forever and half-knows it; the static on her station is your fading memory of her voice. Every song on KNWH cuts off at the exact bar where you stopped knowing it, because the radio in your house was switched off the day the news came.
cast.reactsToMemory: If you sing along with her broadcasts night after night, her leitmotif gains an instrument each time and her text renders more solid; if you avoid the station, her signal audibly weakens act by act until her dialogue prints so faint you must raise Clarity just to read her. She also remembers every dedication you ever made and will quote your own words back to you, on air, at the worst and best possible moment.

cast.name: Elias Pike
cast.role: Cartographer and lamplighter who walks the shrinking border every night, redrawing the town's map by hand. Gentle, obsessive, apologetic. Your guide to the fog's rules.
cast.want: To finish a complete map of Meridian before the fog does — he believes a fully drawn town cannot be unmade.
cast.secret: He erases as much as he draws. The fog pays him for surrendered streets in returned scraps of his own past, and he has been trading pieces of the town for pieces of himself for years. And there is a hole in the center of his map — one lot he has never once managed to draw: your house.
cast.reactsToMemory: If you walked the border with him in Act I, he entrusts the map to you in Act III (required for one ending); if you instead exposed his trades publicly, he either shatters or is freed depending on your Warmth at that moment — and either way, every street he traded away while you knew and said nothing is listed, in his handwriting, on the finale's map printout.

cast.name: Dora Vex
cast.role: Owner of Vex Sundries (pharmacy, diner, upright piano played badly on purpose). The town skeptic and truth-teller. The player's route to learning the twist early.
cast.want: For everyone to stop pretending. She wants the town to face the fade with open eyes, even if open eyes are the end of it.
cast.secret: She remembers arriving in Meridian the way you did — because she is a fragment, an amalgam of two people you knew only slightly (your piano teacher and the pharmacist), which is why her biography contradicts itself and why she feels 'wrong' to everyone including herself. Her deliberately bad piano is your bad memory of the lessons.
cast.reactsToMemory: She is the game's callback engine: she quotes, verbatim, throwaway things you said nights ago ('You told me Tuesday you don't believe in the surveyor. Sit down.'). If your Nerve is high enough by Act II, she is the only character who will tell you the truth to your face — and whether you thanked her or fled that conversation permanently changes how she plays the piano in the finale: badly, or, once, correctly.

cast.name: The Dog (Biscuit, or Blue, or Bones — depends who you ask)
cast.role: The town's dog. Everyone remembers it differently; its name changes with the speaker. Leads you to thin places. The game's smallest and oldest wound.
cast.want: To be found. To be called by one name and answered to it.
cast.secret: It is the oldest memory in Meridian and your first grief — the dog you left behind in 1974, the goodbye you never said. It is the only resident who has always known you.
cast.reactsToMemory: It follows you only if you fed it, and it remembers being fed across the entire game — one meal on night one buys you a companion in the ninth room on night nine. In the 'Kept' ending you are asked, at last, to give it its real name; the game accepts only one answer, and the answer is discoverable from clues, not told.

mystery: As the player experiences it, the mystery has four braided strands that each look answerable and each open onto the next. (1) THE FADE: why is the fog taking Meridian street by street, why does it obey rules (calendars stuck, songs cut off mid-bridge, a lot no one can draw), and why has it accelerated the night you arrived? (2) THE GUEST: who are you? You have a ticket you didn't buy, a suitcase that isn't yours whose contents keep matching stories residents tell BEFORE they tell them, and no one — not once, in the whole game — says your name. (3) THE SCHEDULE: what does the 11:47 want with you? It has no arrivals board, only departures; the Conductor keeps consulting a ledger and a watch, and he is visibly spending something to keep you here. (4) THE WAITING: why did everyone in town seem to be expecting you specifically — and why does their hope feel like grief? The town believes you are the surveyor come at last to put Meridian back on the map. The player is invited to believe it too, and the game's verbs support it: you can map, catalog, record, and 'keep' things. The dread that accumulates is that surveying isn't saving — that you are not here to write the town down but to carry it out, and that the fade and your arrival are not coincidence but the same event.
twist: You are not a visitor. You are the last person alive who remembers Meridian. The town was bypassed, drowned in paperwork, and abandoned decades ago; it has persisted since only in the pooled memory of the people who lived there, shrinking a little each time one of them died. Now you — the child who left on the morning train in June 1974, after your sister Wren died, without saying goodbye to your dog — are dying, old, the final rememberer. The 11:47 is your last night crossing; Mr. Sett is the psychopomp who bent his schedule to grant you nine nights of stopover in the place you spent a lifetime not thinking about. Every resident is a memory who doesn't know it: your mother at 34, your sister frozen at fifteen, amalgams like Dora where your memory blurred two people into one. The fog is not an invader — it is you, forgetting, in real time; it accelerated when you arrived because arriving here means you are at the end. Nobody says your name because memories cannot produce what the rememberer lost first, and names go first. The suitcase is yours — packed by your mother the day you left, unopened for sixty years. The game's question was never 'can you save the town.' Meridian cannot be saved. The question the entire final act asks is the one the Conductor puts to you on the platform: 'The luggage allowance is one heart. What will you carry?'
cluesInRetrospect: (1) EVERY CALENDAR IN TOWN READS JUNE 1974 — remarked on early as quaint small-town neglect; it is the month you left, the moment your Meridian stopped updating. (2) THE SONGS CUT OFF MID-BRIDGE — every record Wren spins on KNWH dies at an exact bar, always the same bar per song, which the town treats as 'the fog eating the grooves.' It's the point at which you stopped knowing each song: the radio in your house went silent the day Wren died. On replay this clue is devastating — and one ending requires learning a song PAST its cut, from other characters humming fragments. (3) NO ONE EVER SAYS YOUR NAME — 'guest,' 'kiddo,' 'love.' Played as small-town manner. Memories can't speak what the rememberer has lost, and names go first. The save-name you typed at the start is finally spoken aloud exactly once, in one ending, by Mercy. (4) THE NINTH DOOR IS DRAWN IN PENCIL — the Anchor & Lantern has nine rooms but room nine's door is a sketch on the wall, which Mercy waves off as 'never got finished.' It is your childhood bedroom; you cannot remember it, so it cannot exist — yet. (5) YOUR REFLECTION IN VEX'S WINDOW IS A CHILD — Dora says 'mirrors are honest here' and everyone laughs. Terminal build renders your reflection's dialogue in a smaller font. (6) NO ARRIVALS, ONLY DEPARTURES — the station board lists departures only; Elias's map has one blank lot at the center 'he never gets around to'; the suitcase's contents (a tin whistle, a child's drawing of a dog, one unpunched return ticket dated June 1974) each surface BEFORE the story that explains them, so the reveal lands as recognition, not information.
actStructure: PROLOGUE — THE 11:47 (one scene): arrival; the Ticket Interview with Mr. Sett (LLM-assisted character creation, deterministic fallback); first sight of the pop. "—" sign. ACT I — NINE STREETS (nights 1–3): learn the town's routines; watch the fog take Third Street on-screen (text physically fades as residents chat through it). Hard branch: THE KEEPING — you commit your nights to one of three keepers: Mercy's lamp (Warmth route), Wren's signal (music route), or Elias's map (Clarity route). This choice sets your primary companion, which district survives longest, which leitmotif becomes your Act III accompaniment, and roughly 40% of Act II content. Small choices (feeding the dog, washing dishes) silently set flags the finale depends on. ACT II — THIN PLACES (nights 4–6): investigation phase; the suitcase starts matching stories; Weight admits you into faded streets where 'kept' fragments persist. Two hard branches: (a) DORA'S TRUTH — with sufficient Nerve you can force the truth from Dora in Act II, learning the twist MID-GAME. This forks the entire back half: you may tell the town (residents learn they are memories — catastrophe or liberation depending on your Warmth, leading toward the 'Last Dance' ending) or carry it alone (Static climbs every night you conceal it). (b) THE THROUGH TICKET — the Conductor offers early boarding; accepting ends the game in Act II (a real, complete, cold ending — the town fades mid-sentence, literally). ACT III — THE NINTH ROOM (nights 7–8): the fog reaches the square; the pencil door can be opened only with tokens earned per relationship (Mercy's note, Wren's bridge, Elias's map, the dog's trust — you will not have all of them on a first run). Inside is the memory you refused: the morning you left. Branch: who stands with you at the door, and whether you look (Nerve) or turn away (Static). FINALE — THE PLATFORM (night 9): the 11:47 comes for the last time. All routes converge here and diverge again: the Conductor's ledger, your Static, your kept tokens, and the Dora fork resolve into one of seven endings. If the twist was never learned, it detonates HERE — Mercy packing your suitcase the same way it was packed the first time.

endings.name: Kept (the true ending)
endings.howReached: Open the ninth room bearing all four tokens (Mercy's missing note, Wren's completed bridge, Elias's map, the fed and trusted dog), face the memory with high Nerve, keep Static under 25, and on the platform answer the Conductor's question by naming — out loud, from clues — the dog's real name. Effectively requires knowledge from a prior run or near-perfect play.
endings.tone: Devastating and warm. Mercy speaks your save-name aloud — the only time in the game anyone says it. You board carrying the whole town as a song: the score plays every leitmotif in counterpoint and resolves its withheld suspension for the first time in the entire game. The terminal prints Elias's map one final time, complete, with your house drawn in.

endings.name: The Through Ticket
endings.howReached: Accept the Conductor's Act II offer of early boarding, refusing the stopover you were given.
endings.tone: Numb, merciful, deliberately abrupt. Meridian fades mid-sentence — the closing text literally cuts off unfinished, and the credits run in silence except for wheels on rails.

endings.name: The Lamplighter's Bargain
endings.howReached: Learn Elias's secret, then take his trade yourself: give the fog your memories of the world outside in exchange for keeping Meridian lit. You stay. The town keeps you instead of you keeping it.
endings.tone: Eerie, bittersweet, quietly wrong in a way that grows on reflection. The game 'forgets' your save name — the file itself is renamed to 'the lamplighter.' On a later run, other residents mention the new lamplighter who 'came from away.'

endings.name: Static
endings.howReached: Reach Static 75+ by persistent lying, forcing, and looking away; the fog takes you before it takes the town.
endings.tone: Horror-adjacent tragedy. Final scenes render in progressive glyph-rot; your last dialogue options are corrupted beyond choosing; the epilogue is the residents, untouched, wondering aloud about the guest who never came.

endings.name: Broadcast
endings.howReached: Commit fully to Wren's route: sing with her every night, gather the missing bars of one song from residents' hummed fragments across all three acts, then perform the whole song — past its cut — into the live transmitter on night 8.
endings.tone: Small, piercing catharsis. Her signal gets through — one caller, sixty years away and long ago, a child turning a radio back on. You board carrying only her voice. The credits song is her station sign-off, finally completed.

endings.name: The Last Dance
endings.howReached: Force Dora's truth in Act II with high Nerve, then — with high Warmth — choose to tell the town what it is. Instead of fading in denial, Meridian throws one final dance in the square.
endings.tone: Luminous and defiant. Every character knowingly says goodbye; the score plays all leitmotifs as a single waltz; Dora plays the piano correctly, once, at last. Grief with the lights on.

endings.name: The Empty Seat (secret)
endings.howReached: Convince the Conductor to depart without you AND refuse Elias's bargain: you attempt to remain in Meridian as yourself, unpaid-for, against the rules.
endings.tone: Quietly uncanny. You become the town's new blank lot — the thing on the map no one can draw. Seeds cross-run awareness: in New Game Plus, residents mention someone who 'stays out past the border,' and your previous save-name appears, faded, on the station's departures board as CANCELLED.

musicIdentity: THE STRUCTURAL DEVICE: the entire score is built from one six-note motif ('the Meridian line') that ends on an unresolved suspension — the melody itself never comes home. Every cue in the game withholds the tonic. It resolves exactly once, in the 'Kept' ending, which means players FEEL the resolution in their spine without knowing why: the game has been teaching their ear to ache for that chord for six hours. PER-CHARACTER LEITMOTIFS (all variations of the six notes, so every combination is automatically coherent): MERCY — the motif as a hearth-warm 3/4 folk waltz, upright piano and accordion, always mid-phrase when you enter the inn, as if it never stopped. WREN — the motif inverted, square-wave and tape static, arranged like a station ident; it gains one real instrument every night you sing with her and loses one every night you don't. ELIAS — the motif in canon with itself, plucked strings, the second voice one bar behind the first (the map lags the town); when he trades a street away, the trailing voice drops out for a measure. MR. SETT — the motif flattened into a clock-regular muted-brass pulse at exactly 74 bpm (June '74), which the player's ear learns to associate with time running out; the finale platform scene is scored at 74 bpm no matter which ending. THE DOG — the motif whistled on a tin whistle, always missing its final note until Mercy's token supplies it. THE FOG — the motif time-stretched 8x into a drone, pitched down as Static rises. RECOMBINATION AT STORY BEATS: your Act I 'keeping' choice decides whose variation accompanies the main theme in the ninth room (companion's motif left hand, Meridian line right hand); 'The Last Dance' arranges all six variations as one waltz; the 'Kept' finale plays them in full counterpoint and finally lands the tonic. ADAPTIVE + DUAL-PLATFORM BY DESIGN: the score is authored once as tracker/MIDI-style note data with layer flags, rendered by one engine with two instrument banks — browser gets WebAudio with sampled felt piano, accordion, tape-warped strings and vinyl noise; terminal gets the same note data through a chip-synth voice (square/triangle/noise channels via rodio/miniaudio) — diegetically, the terminal build is Meridian heard through KNWH's transmitter, so the 'AM radio chiptune version' is canon, not a compromise. Static bitcrushes and detunes both banks in real time; location crossfades motifs; faded streets get faded arrangements (fewer voices, more air)."
llmTouchpoints: Three touchpoints, each narrow, schema-validated, and fully playable without any LLM. (1) THE TICKET INTERVIEW (character creation): Mr. Sett asks five authored questions ('What did mornings smell like, where you're from?'). An LLM maps freeform answers onto the four stats and extracts exactly three 'memory tokens' — concrete nouns, validated against a length/content whitelist — which are slot-inserted verbatim into authored scenes later (your smell-of-home is waiting inside the ninth room). The LLM never writes prose; it classifies and extracts. FALLBACK: each question offers curated multiple-choice answers wired to the same stat mapping and a token picklist; identical downstream behavior. (2) WREN'S DEDICATIONS: once per night you may dedicate a song on KNWH 'to someone, for something' in your own words. The LLM classifies the dedication into one of twelve authored categories (each with authored consequences and flags) and lightly rewrites it in Wren's on-air voice within a strict template. FALLBACK: keyword-rule classification plus a set of authored dedications; your exact words are still read on air unmodified. (3) ELIAS'S MAP EPITAPHS: when the fog takes a street, you may write its epitaph in the map margin. The LLM does tone classification only (kept / warm / bitter) to set a flag that shifts Elias's arc. Your exact words are echoed in the finale's printed map regardless — that echo is deterministic string storage and needs no model at all. FALLBACK: three authored epitaph templates per street with pre-assigned tones. DESIGN RULE THROUGHOUT: the LLM classifies, extracts, and lightly reformats inside templates; it never generates plot, dialogue trees, or endings. Offline/terminal players lose nothing structural — only the freeform input surface.
emotionalCore: The game is about grief as curation: the specific, adult heartbreak of loving something you cannot keep and having to choose what to carry. Not 'can you save it' — you can't, and the game is honest about that earlier than most games dare — but 'what will you keep, and will you look at it directly while you still can.' Every mechanic is that feeling operationalized: Weight, the tokens, the fading text, the songs that cut off where your attention did. The scene everything is built toward is the ninth room and the platform beyond it — standing in your own unremembered bedroom with whoever you earned the right to stand with, and then Mercy on the platform, packing your suitcase the same way she packed it in 1974, saying: 'You were always going to leave, love. That's what children are for.' And the Conductor, watch open, asking the game's real question: 'The luggage allowance is one heart. What will you carry?' The score resolving its six-hour suspension under that line — that is the moment a stranger reading this pitch should get chills about, and the moment players will not be able to describe to their friends without their voice doing the thing.
replayHook: First-run pull: the Act I 'keeping' choice (lamp / signal / map) swaps your companion, your Act III accompaniment motif, and ~40% of mid-game content, and the Dora truth-fork makes an informed second run a structurally different game (playing Acts II–III while KNOWING is a new experience — dramatic irony as gameplay). Ending gates create honest incompleteness: no first run can hold all four tokens, and 'Kept' practically requires knowledge (the dog's true name, the missing bars of Wren's song) discovered across runs. CROSS-RUN AWARENESS (NG+ as haunting): the game remembers previous playthroughs diegetically — the Conductor's brass ledger carries kindness counts across runs and he remarks 'you've ridden with me before, I think'; KNWH occasionally airs dedications you wrote in earlier runs as 'old requests'; the 'Empty Seat' ending plants your prior save-name on the departures board as CANCELLED; the 'Lamplighter' ending renames your save file and a later run finds your old character living in Meridian as the lamplighter, who does not remember you. The save system is thematic: this is a game about being remembered, and it remembers you.
whyNotUndertaleClone: It steals Undertale's principles and none of its content or systems. No combat, no mercy/fight axis, no monsters, no comedy-forward register: the tone is magical-realist elegy (Kentucky Route Zero, Our Town, spirited-away-at-dusk) rather than subversive RPG pastiche. Where Undertale subverts RPG combat conventions, NOT HERE subverts the conventions of its own media — text, save files, and terminals: prose fades and corrupts, the save name becomes a loaded gun fired once in six hours, an ending renames your file, NG+ is diegetic haunting rather than a difficulty lap. The leitmotif principle is shared, but the musical architecture is its own invention — a single six-note motif whose resolution is withheld for the entire game and granted once. Consequence-memory is shared but reframed: in Undertale the world remembers what you did; here, memory IS the world — every character is literally made of remembering, so 'the game remembers your actions' is not a feature bolted onto the fiction, it is the fiction. And the moral stakes differ at the root: Undertale asks whether you'll spare others; NOT HERE asks what you'll carry when you can't keep anything — a question with no villain, no route named after violence, and no winning state, only better and worse goodbyes.
